brief_document.title: Implement channel metrics for rate, subscribers, queue depth, and latency
brief_document.purpose: Instrument the channel subsystem with the four bus-native metrics that operators need to monitor channel health: message rate (counter per channel), subscriber count (gauge per channel), durable queue depth (gauge), and delivery latency (histogram). These metrics register into the MetricsRegistry from OBS-001 and are recorded non-blockingly on the hot path.
brief_document.task: Create crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs containing the ChannelMetrics struct that registers the four channel metric families with the MetricsRegistry. Provide methods for the channel actor to call on publish, subscribe/unsubscribe, enqueue (durable), and delivery-complete events. Each metric is keyed by channel name as a label. Recording must never block the channel actor's message loop.

requirements.title: Define ChannelMetrics struct
requirements.spec: ChannelMetrics SHALL hold handles to the four metric families: message_rate (Counter), subscriber_count (Gauge), queue_depth (Gauge), delivery_latency (Histogram). ChannelMetrics SHALL be constructed from a MetricsRegistry reference. ChannelMetrics SHALL implement Debug and Clone. ChannelMetrics SHALL NOT store any per-channel state — labels are passed at recording time.
acceptance: ChannelMetrics struct exists in crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs
ChannelMetrics has fields for all four metric handles
ChannelMetrics::new(registry: &MetricsRegistry) registers all four families
ChannelMetrics implements Debug and Clone

requirements.title: Track message rate as a counter per channel
requirements.spec: ChannelMetrics SHALL expose record_publish(channel_name) that increments the message_rate counter for the given channel. The counter SHALL be monotonically increasing and labelled by channel name. The increment SHALL be a single atomic operation. record_publish SHALL NOT return an error — it is fire-and-forget.
acceptance: record_publish(channel_name) increments the counter by 1
The counter label includes the channel name
Calling record_publish 100 times results in counter value 100
record_publish does not return Result — it is infallible

requirements.title: Track subscriber count as a gauge per channel
requirements.spec: ChannelMetrics SHALL expose record_subscribe(channel_name) and record_unsubscribe(channel_name) that increment and decrement the subscriber_count gauge. The gauge SHALL reflect the current number of active subscribers for each channel. Both operations SHALL be atomic.
acceptance: record_subscribe increments the gauge by 1
record_unsubscribe decrements the gauge by 1
After 3 subscribes and 1 unsubscribe, gauge value is 2
Both methods are infallible (no Result return)

requirements.title: Track durable channel queue depth as a gauge
requirements.spec: ChannelMetrics SHALL expose set_queue_depth(channel_name, depth) that sets the queue_depth gauge to the given absolute value. Queue depth applies only to durable channels — ephemeral channels have unbounded fan-out and no queue. The gauge SHALL be labelled by channel name.
acceptance: set_queue_depth(name, 42) sets the gauge to 42
Calling set_queue_depth(name, 0) sets the gauge to 0
The gauge label includes the channel name
set_queue_depth is infallible

requirements.title: Track delivery latency as a histogram per channel
requirements.spec: ChannelMetrics SHALL expose record_delivery_latency(channel_name, duration) that observes the given Duration into the delivery_latency histogram. The histogram SHALL use configurable bucket boundaries provided at construction time. The histogram SHALL be labelled by channel name. SHALL NOT impose default bucket values — buckets are a required parameter to ChannelMetrics::new.
acceptance: record_delivery_latency(name, Duration::from_millis(50)) records an observation
The histogram is labelled by channel name
Bucket boundaries are passed at construction time, not hardcoded
record_delivery_latency is infallible

requirements.title: Add channel module to metrics mod.rs
requirements.spec: crates/liminal/src/metrics/mod.rs SHALL declare pub mod channel and re-export ChannelMetrics.
acceptance: metrics/mod.rs contains pub mod channel
ChannelMetrics is re-exported from the metrics module
cargo check -p liminal passes

requirements.title: Non-blocking recording guarantee
requirements.spec: All ChannelMetrics recording methods SHALL complete without acquiring any Mutex or RwLock. Recording SHALL NOT allocate heap memory on the hot path. Recording SHALL NOT block on I/O. These methods are called inside the channel actor's message loop and SHALL NOT introduce latency into message delivery.
acceptance: No Mutex::lock or RwLock::write appears in any recording method
No Box::new, Vec::push, or String allocation appears in any recording method
No I/O operations appear in any recording method
Recording a metric completes in O(1) time

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the Prometheus text rendering — that is OBS-005.
SHALL NOT implement pressure-related metrics (reject, defer) — that is OBS-004.
SHALL NOT modify the channel actor itself — metrics are recorded by calling ChannelMetrics methods from the actor, not by changing actor internals.
SHALL NOT impose default histogram bucket boundaries — they are caller-supplied.
SHALL NOT add persistence or durability logic — queue depth is reported, not managed.
verification: cargo clippy -p liminal -- -D warnings passes clean.
All recording methods are infallible (no Result return type).
No Mutex or RwLock acquisition in any recording method.
Every metric is labelled by channel name.

adrs.title: Backpressure as a wire protocol primitive with accept/defer/reject signals
adrs.decision: Backpressure is part of the wire protocol with three signals: accept (delivered), defer (buffered, will deliver when capacity frees), reject (consumer overwhelmed, message shed). Consumers declare capacity. The bus mediates between producer rate and consumer capacity. The rejected alternative is Kafka-style consumer-lag-only backpressure (not actionable by producers in real time).

checklist.text: Channel message rate tracked as a counter per channel, incremented on each successful publish.
Channel subscriber count tracked as a gauge per channel, reflecting current live subscribers.
Durable channel queue depth tracked as a gauge per channel, reflecting messages awaiting acknowledgement.
Channel delivery latency tracked as a histogram per channel, measuring time from publish call to subscriber delivery.

stories.text: As an Operator, I want to see per-channel message rates so that I can identify hot channels and plan capacity accordingly.
As an Operator, I want to see per-channel delivery latency so that I can detect degradation before it impacts consumers.
As an Application Developer, I want durable channel queue depth visible as a metric so that I can monitor consumer lag and tune my processing capacity.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. Extract into submodules if approaching the limit.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
Metrics instrumentation points in channel and conversation actors must not block the actor's message processing. Recording a metric is a non-blocking operation.
Trace context propagation is transparent to application code. No manual context passing required for intra-bus messages.
Prometheus export renders a consistent snapshot. Metrics do not change mid-render -- the registry provides an atomic read.
Structured log entries never contain free-form string messages. Every field is a typed key-value pair.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, an operator can see inside the running bus: which channels are hot, which consumers are under pressure, which conversations are stalling, and how messages flow across a multi-step exchange. The observability layer does not change how the bus works -- it makes the bus's existing behaviour visible.

The goal is operational confidence. An operator should be able to capacity-plan from metrics, diagnose delivery issues from traces, and correlate structured logs across conversation boundaries. An application developer should be able to trace a message through a conversation chain without instrumenting their own code.
